coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical assay of protein kinase activity based on the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ functionalized PDMS microchip
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Rui-Han
Chen, Xiao-Juan
Chen, Juan
Liang, Ru-Ping
Qiu, Jian-Ding
coredata.dc:description: A new strategy for the sensitive assay of protein kinase activity has been constructed using Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ nanocomposite as stationary phase in microchip electrophoresis. Through the self-polymerization of norepinephrine in weak alkaline solution, a thin poly(norepinephrine) (PNE) film can be spontaneously formed on the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) to produce Fe3O4@PNE NPs. Due to the good magnetic property, Fe3O4@PNE NPs can be easily fixed in poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) channel under external magnetic field. On the other hand, the catechol hydroxyl groups in PNE can chelate Ti4+ to form Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ in PDMS channel. When peptides are phosphorylated by protein kinase A (PKA) in the presence of adenosine 5′-triphosphate (ATP), the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed in PDMS channels can specifically coordinate with the phosphorylated sites of phosphopeptides, but the non-phosphorylated peptides cannot bind with Ti4+. Thus, the retention time of the phosphorylated peptides in the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS channel was increased, and phosphorylated peptides and non-phosphorylated peptides can be separated successfully. Due to the high loading of Ti4+ on the Fe3O4@PNE, this strategy can be used for the enrichment of phosphorylated peptides and electrochemical assay of PKA activity. This method can also be used for quantitative analysis of PKA activity in cell lysate, indicating its potential application in clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ nanocomposite
Protein kinase activity
Microchip electrophoresis
Phosphorylated peptide
Electrochemical assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS microchannel
EOF of Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs coated microchip
Separation of phosphorylated peptide and non-phosphorylated peptide

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) Fe3O4 NPs and (B) Fe3O4@PNE NPs.

(A) FTIR spectra of (a) Fe3O4 NPs, (b) norepinephrine, (c) PNE, (d) Fe3O4@PNE NPs, (e) Ti(SO4)2, and (f) Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs. (B) Zeta potentials of Fe3O4 NPs, Fe3O4@PNE NPs, and Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs. (C) The contact angles of (a) PDMS, (b) Fe3O4@PNE packed PDMS, and (c) Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS.

EOF-pH curves on (a) PDMS and (b) Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS microchannels. Conditions: running buffer, 50 mM Tris-HCl; separation voltage, +1100 V; injection voltage, +800 V; injection time, 5 s; detection potential, 0 V.

Separation of phosphorylated peptide and non-phosphorylated peptide on (a) PDMS microchip, (b) Fe3O4@PNE packed PDMS microchip, and (c) Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS microchip. Separation of (d) peptide containing ATP and (e) peptide containing PKA on Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS microchip. Conditions: running buffer, 50 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0; separation voltage, +1100 V; detection potential, +0.6 V.

(A) Electrophoretogram of phosphorylated peptide and non-phosphorylated peptide on the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS microchip at different concentrations of PKA (from a to h: 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 and 4.0 U/mL). (B) Relationship between the lg(A2/(A1 + A2)) and the lgCPKA. Other conditions are the same as in Fig. 4.
Scheme of the preparation of Fe3O4@PNE NPs and the protein kinase activity assay on Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs packed microchannel.
PKA activity measured in cell lysates with the as-proposed assay.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical assay of protein kinase activity based on the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ functionalized PDMS microchip

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Pingxiang University, Pingxiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Pingxiang University, Pingxiang, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new strategy for the sensitive assay of protein kinase activity has been constructed using Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ nanocomposite as stationary phase in microchip electrophoresis. Through the self-polymerization of norepinephrine in weak alkaline solution, a thin poly(norepinephrine) (PNE) film can be spontaneously formed on the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) to produce Fe3O4@PNE NPs. Due to the good magnetic property, Fe3O4@PNE NPs can be easily fixed in poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) channel under external magnetic field. On the other hand, the catechol hydroxyl groups in PNE can chelate Ti4+ to form Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ in PDMS channel. When peptides are phosphorylated by protein kinase A (PKA) in the presence of adenosine 5′-triphosphate (ATP), the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed in PDMS channels can specifically coordinate with the phosphorylated sites of phosphopeptides, but the non-phosphorylated peptides cannot bind with Ti4+. Thus, the retention time of the phosphorylated peptides in the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS channel was increased, and phosphorylated peptides and non-phosphorylated peptides can be separated successfully. Due to the high loading of Ti4+ on the Fe3O4@PNE, this strategy can be used for the enrichment of phosphorylated peptides and electrochemical assay of PKA activity. This method can also be used for quantitative analysis of PKA activity in cell lysate, indicating its potential application in clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Protein kinase A (PKA) is a kind of protein kinase, aberrations expression of PKA activity and abnormal phosphorylation states are closely associated with chronic inflammatory disease [1], immune deficiencies [2], diabetes, cancer [3], and the like. Radiometric assays are generally applicable for protein kinase detection, while the radioactive labels suffer from the inherent drawback of being harmful to the environment and human health due to the use of radioactive adenosine triphosphate [4]. To eliminate the problem, several techniques including electrogenerated chemiluminescence [5–7], resonance light scattering [8], fluorescence [9], mass spectrometry [10], electrochemical [11,12], etc. have been developed. Although these methods show high specificity and rapidity [13], one of the main difficulties is that phosphopeptides are difficult to detect without enrichment due to the low abundance. Separation of the phosphorylated product from the non-phosphorylated substrate by mass spectrometry [14] or capillary electrophoresis [15] provides an effective way to assay the protein kinase activity. Recently, various materials and techniques have been used for selective enrichment of phosphorylated peptide. Capillary electrophoresis [16–19] and microchip electrophoresis [20] have also been utilized for assay of kinase activity or inhibitors. Ma et al. synthesized magnetic composite microsphere for identifying low-abundant phosphopeptide from biological samples [21]. Deng et al. synthesized Ti4+-Fe3O4@PDA for sensitive enrichment of phosphopeptide [22].

Norepinephrine is known as a mimic of the adhesive protein [23], poly(norepinephrine) (PNE) film can be formed on virtually any material surface through the self-polymerization of norepinephrine [24]. Owing to the presence of catechol hydroxyl groups, the hydrophilicity of the PNE functionalized surface can be improved efficiently, and moreover, the PNE coating can chelate with Ti4+ ions, which implies that Ti4+ can be immobilized on various substrates modified by PNE. Microchips have been used to separate target peptides for protein kinase assay [25]. Maeda et al. described microchip-based phosphate-affinity electrophoresis for separation of peptide substrate and phosphorylated peptide [26]. Chen et al. utilized Fe3+-immobilized magnetic nanoparticles coated capillary to separate phosphoproteins and nonphosphoproteins [27].

Herein, a core-shell Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ was facilely synthesized and used for efficient enrichment of phosphorylated peptides and sensitive assay of protein kinase activity. A thin PNE layer was firstly coated on the magnetic Fe3O4 particles through self-polymerization of norepinephrine. The Fe3O4@PNE NPs were then packed in the poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) microchannel under external magnetic field. With pumping of Ti4+ solution into the Fe3O4@PNE packed PDMS channel, catechol hydroxyl groups in PNE can chelate Ti4+ to form Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS channel (Scheme 1 ). When peptides are phosphorylated by PKA in the presence of ATP, efficient separation of phosphorylated peptides and non-phosphorylated peptides can be observed on the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs packed microchip due to the specific interaction between phosphate groups and Ti4+. The higher the activity of protein kinase, the more phosphorylated peptide and the greater peak area of phosphorylated peptide can be detected. Therefore, quantitative, fast and sensitive assay of PKA activity can be realized on the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs based microchip.
Chemicals, Characterization, Fabrication of PDMS microchip, Electrochemical assay, and Preparation of cell lysates were presented in Supplementary Material.

Fe3O4 NPs were firstly synthesized by a solvothermal reaction [28]. Then, 25 mg of Fe3O4 NPs were added in 10 mL of Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 8.5) and mechanical stirred to form a uniform suspension of Fe3O4 NPs. Subsequently, 30 mg norepinephrine hydrochloride was added and continually stirred for 24 h at room temperature. The resulting product was collected with a magnet and thoroughly washed with ultrapure water for several times, and the magnetic Fe3O4@PNE NPs were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS microchannel
ce:section.ce:para: The uniform suspension of Fe3O4@PNE NPs was agitated in the separation reservoir and then pumped into the PDMS microchannel by vacuum for 5 min, and the excessive solution was flushed out of the channel with running buffer. The Fe3O4@PNE NPs were packed in the channel at a region where the magnets were located. Subsequently, 100 mM Ti(SO4)2 solution was continuously pumped into the Fe3O4@PNE NPs packed PDMS separation channel for 5 min and rest for 4 h, the microchannel was then rinsed with water for 5 min and the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs packed PDMS microchannel was obtained (Scheme 1).

In a series of phosphorylation reaction system (100 μL), 0.25 mM peptide substrate was incubated with different concentrations of PKA and 0.5 mM ATP in 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.0) containing 10 mM MgCl2 and 10 mM KCl at 37 °C for 1 h.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was utilized to investigate the morphology and size of the synthesized Fe3O4 NPs and Fe3O4@PNE NPs. The Fe3O4 NPs were sphere with a mean diameter of 122 nm (Fig. 1A). After self-polymerization of norepinephrine on Fe3O4 NPs surface, the formed Fe3O4@PNE NPs retained spherical structure with average diameter of 142 nm (Fig. 1B), which indicates that the PNE layer on Fe3O4 NPs surface was about 10 nm. The particle size distribution was uniform, which is beneficial to obtain a permeable and uniform packing of the nanoparticles in the microchannel. The magnetic property of the Fe3O4@PNE NPs was investigated by comparing Fe3O4@PNE NPs suspension with and without magnet. When a magnet was closed to the Fe3O4@PNE NPs suspension, Fe3O4@PNE NPs were attracted quickly toward the magnet. The results confirmed the good magnetic capture and separation ability of the synthesized Fe3O4@PNE NPs.

As shown in Fig. 2A, the infrared spectrum of Fe3O4 NPs (curve a) shows a strong absorption peak of FeO group at 577 cm−1 [29]. The norepinephrine spectrum showed the stretching vibration peaks of NH group of the primary amine and catechol −OH group at about 3310 cm−1 and 3100 cm−1 (curve b). When norepinephrine self-polymerized to PNE (curve c), horseshoe peak of primary amine at 3310 cm−1 was nearly disappeared, the stretching vibrations of NH group of secondary amine and catechol −OH group exhibited at the range of 3450 cm−1 - 3100 cm−1, and the CC resonance vibrations in the benzene ring and the C−N stretching modes appeared at 1615 cm−1 and 1514 cm−1, respectively [30]. The spectrum of Fe3O4@PNE NPs shows the absorption peaks of both PNE and Fe3O4 NPs (curve d), indicating the successful coating of PNE on the Fe3O4 NPs surface and the structure of Fe3O4 NPs was maintained well. After chelation of Ti4+ on Fe3O4@PNE NPs, the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs (curve f) exhibited the Ti(SO4)2 absorption peaks (curve e) of the SO stretching vibration of free SO4 2− at 1210 cm−1 and 1045 cm−1, and the absorption peaks at about 1644 cm−1 were related to the bending vibration of the hydroxyl. The absorption peak around 520–580 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of TiO [31,32]. The results demonstrated that the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs were formed by chelation between PNE and Ti4+ [25].

The zeta potential experiments also demonstrated the formation of the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs (Fig. 2B). The zeta potential (ζ) of Fe3O4 NPs was −15.8 mV, while the ζ of Fe3O4@PNE NPs decreased to −28.3 mV due to the plentiful catechol and amine functional group in PNE [33,34]. Binding of the Ti4+ ions to the Fe3O4@PNE NPs surface increased the ζ to 17.1 mV, due to the chelation of catechol hydroxyl group of PNE with the metal counterion (Ti4+) [35]. These results indicated that Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs can be successfully obtained by the proposed strategy.

As shown in Fig. 2C, the contact angles of the PDMS, Fe3O4@PNE NPs packed PDMS, and Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs packed PDMS were 110° (a), 28° (b), and 11° (c), respectively. Compared with the PDMS and Fe3O4@PNE NPs packed PDMS, the surface wettability of the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs packed PDMS was distinctly improved, which facilitated filling of the PDMS microchannel with aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EOF of Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs coated microchip
ce:section.ce:para: Electroosmotic flow (EOF) of the PDMS microchips before and after modification of Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs on the pH range of 3–11 was measured (Fig. 3 ). The EOF was 2.37 × 10−4 cm2 V−1 s−1 at pH 8.0 and showed obvious pH-dependent tendency on the PDMS microchip (curve a). After packing of Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs, the EOF decreased to 1.61 × 10−4 cm2 V−1 s−1 and exhibited weaker pH-dependent tendency (curve b). Relative standard deviation of EOF on the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs packed PDMS microchip (0.48%, n = 5) was nearly 1/7 of that on the PDMS microchip (3.4%, n = 5), indicated the good stability of the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs packed PDMS microchip.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of phosphorylated peptide and non-phosphorylated peptide
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the separation efficiency of phosphorylated peptide and non-phosphorylated peptide on PDMS microchip, Fe3O4@PNE packed PDMS microchip, and Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS microchip. It is clear that the separation of phosphorylated peptide and non-phosphorylated peptide in PDMS microchip (curve a) and Fe3O4@PNE packed PDMS microchip (curve b) cannot be achieved. Baseline separation of phosphorylated peptide and non-phosphorylated peptide with the elution time of phosphorylated peptide > non-phosphorylated peptide on Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS microchip can be observed (curve c). The resolution factor was about 1.12, and the theoretical plate numbers were estimated to be 2.31 × 104 plates/m and 2.85 × 104 plates/m for phosphorylated peptide and non-phosphorylated peptide, respectively. When peptides mixed with ATP in the absence of PKA (curve d) or peptides mixed with PKA in the absence of ATP (curve e), the phosphorylation reaction of the peptides cannot be occurred, and thus, only the peak of non-phosphorylated peptide can be observed on the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS microchip.

Separation voltage and detection potential for phosphorylated peptide and non-phosphorylated peptide on the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS microchip were optimized (Fig. S1). Under the optimal conditions (detection potential of +0.6 V and separation voltage of +1100 V), the quantitative determination of PKA activity on the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs packed PDMS microchip was investigated (Fig. 5 ). With increasing the concentration of PKA, the peak area of phosphorylated peptide was increased and the peak area of non-phosphorylated peptide was decreased (Fig. 5A). The logarithmic value of peak area ratio of the phosphorylated peptide (A2) and the sum of non-phosphorylated peptide (A1) and phosphorylated peptide (A2) was linearly depended on the logarithm of PKA concentration. The linear range of PKA was measured to be 0.05–4 U/mL (Fig. 5B). The limit of detection was measured to be 0.015 U/mL (3σ), which was lower than those of 0.03 U/mL on DNA-assembled Au NPs polymeric network block signal amplification-based electrochemical biosensor [36], 0.15 U/mL by electrochemical sensing strategy based on zirconium cation mediated signal transition [37], and 2 U/mL by a microchip electrolyte insulator semiconductor sensor for the capacitive detection of PKA [38].

To investigate potential application of the method in biological samples, a desired amount of PKA solution was added in cell lysates. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries were in the range of 97–102% for measurement of 0.1, 1.0 and 4.0 U/mL PKA spiked in cell lysates, and the RSDs were no more than 2.6%, suggesting a great promise of the as-proposed method in practical applications.

In summary, a newly Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs packed PDMS microchip system was designed for the sensitive assay of protein kinase A activity. Different chelation ability of phosphorylated peptide and non-phosphorylated peptide with Ti4+ leads to different retention time of them in the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ NPs packed PDMS microchip. Thus, phosphorylated peptide and non-phosphorylated peptide can be successfully separated and detected. Moreover, the Fe3O4@PNE-Ti4+ packed PDMS microchip exhibited remarkable enrichment ability for phosphorylated peptide, strong recognition ability for phosphorylated peptide and non-phosphorylated peptide, and excellent stability, which provided a powerful protocol for fast and sensitive assay of PKA activity in biological samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly appreciate the supports of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675078, 21775065 and 21976077) and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform Project of Jiangxi Province (20192BCD40001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2020.114645.
